title: Buhl Bull Pup

infobox.value: * [CA-3 Junior/Sport Airsedan](/wiki/Buhl_Airsedan "Buhl Airsedan") * [CA-5 Airsedan](/wiki/Buhl_Airsedan "Buhl Airsedan") * [CA-6 Standard Airsedan](/wiki/Buhl_Airsedan "Buhl Airsedan") * [CA-8 Senior Airsedan](/wiki/Buhl_Airsedan "Buhl Airsedan")
* [CA-3 Airster](/wiki/Buhl-Verville_CA-3_Airster "Buhl-Verville CA-3 Airster") * [J4 Airster](/wiki/Buhl-Verville_CA-3_Airster "Buhl-Verville CA-3 Airster")
* [CA-1 Airster](/wiki/Buhl_CA-1_Airster "Buhl CA-1 Airster") * LA-1 Bull Pup

was a light sports airplane developed in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") in 1930. It was a mid-wing wire-braced [monoplane](/wiki/Monoplane "Monoplane") with fixed [tailskid undercarriage](/wiki/Conventional_landing_gear "Conventional landing gear") and an open [cockpit](/wiki/Cockpit "Cockpit") for the pilot. Buhl developed the Bull Pup as a cheap aircraft through which the company hoped to remain in business as the onset of the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") was felt. However, as the economic situation worsened, it became evident that there was no demand for even such a basic aircraft; when production ceased in 1932, all aircraft still in stock were sold off at half price as the company folded.
* **LA-1A Bull Pup** – version for competition flying with 28 ft wingspan
– version for high-altitude flying with 32 ft wingspan
1 × [Szekely SR-3](/wiki/Szekely_SR-3 "Szekely SR-3") three-cylinder [radial engine](/wiki/Radial_engine "Radial engine"), 45 hp (34 kW)

first_paragraph: The **Buhl LA-1 Bull Pup** was a light sports airplane developed in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") in 1930. It was a mid-wing wire-braced [monoplane](/wiki/Monoplane "Monoplane") with fixed [tailskid undercarriage](/wiki/Conventional_landing_gear "Conventional landing gear") and an open [cockpit](/wiki/Cockpit "Cockpit") for the pilot. Buhl developed the Bull Pup as a cheap aircraft through which the company hoped to remain in business as the onset of the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") was felt. However, as the economic situation worsened, it became evident that there was no demand for even such a basic aircraft; when production ceased in 1932, all aircraft still in stock were sold off at half price as the company folded.

The **Buhl LA-1 Bull Pup** was a light sports airplane developed in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") in 1930. It was a mid-wing wire-braced [monoplane](/wiki/Monoplane "Monoplane") with fixed [tailskid undercarriage](/wiki/Conventional_landing_gear "Conventional landing gear") and an open [cockpit](/wiki/Cockpit "Cockpit") for the pilot. Buhl developed the Bull Pup as a cheap aircraft through which the company hoped to remain in business as the onset of the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") was felt. However, as the economic situation worsened, it became evident that there was no demand for even such a basic aircraft; when production ceased in 1932, all aircraft still in stock were sold off at half price as the company folded.

## Variants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Buhl_Bull_Pup&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Variants")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/BuhlPupInFlight.jpg/250px-BuhlPupInFlight.jpg)](/wiki/File:BuhlPupInFlight.jpg)

Bull Pup c.1936

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/BuhlPup-1936-5-TedLowe-JoePawlicki.jpg/250px-BuhlPup-1936-5-TedLowe-JoePawlicki.jpg)](/wiki/File:BuhlPup-1936-5-TedLowe-JoePawlicki.jpg)

Ted Lowe and Joe Palwicki with a Bull Pup, May 1936

* **LA-1 Bull Pup**
* **LA-1A Bull Pup** – version for competition flying with 28 ft wingspan
* **LA-1B Bull Pup** – version for high-altitude flying with 32 ft wingspan
* **LA-1S Bull Pup** – floatplane version

## Specifications (L-1)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Buhl_Bull_Pup&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Specifications (L-1)")]

*Data from* *Aviation*[[2]](#cite_note-avwk731_p_426-0-2)

**General characteristics**

* **Crew:** 1
* **Length:** 19 ft 0 in (5.79 m)
* **Wingspan:** 30 ft 0 in (9.14 m)
* **Height:** 6 ft 6 in (1.98 m)
* **Wing area:** 109.4 sq ft (10.16 m2)
* **Empty weight:** 550 lb (249 kg)
* **Gross weight:** 850 lb (386 kg)
* **Fuel capacity:** 10 US gal (8.3 imp gal; 38 L)
* **Powerplant:** 1 × [Szekely SR-3](/wiki/Szekely_SR-3 "Szekely SR-3") three-cylinder [radial engine](/wiki/Radial_engine "Radial engine"), 45 hp (34 kW)

**Performance**

* **Maximum speed:** 98 mph (158 km/h, 85 kn)
* **Cruise speed:** 78 mph (126 km/h, 68 kn)
* **Range:** 260 mi (420 km, 230 nmi)
* **Service ceiling:** 14,000 ft (4,300 m)
* **Rate of climb:** 780 ft/min (4.0 m/s)
